#  From AFE to relaxor AFE…

The transition from a conventional antiferroelectric (AFE) to a relaxor AFE state is fundamentally driven by the progressive disruption of long-range dipolar order through compositional engineering. As demonstrated by phase-field simulations and atomic-scale imaging, the pristine PH ceramic exhibits a classic double P–E loop characteristic of long-range ordered AFE systems, where large, uniformly oriented domains switch slowly under an electric field, resulting in substantial hysteresis losses (Fig. 1a). With the introduction of Cd dopants, local compositional inhomogeneities—arising from mismatches in atomic size, mass, and electronegativity—begin to fragment this order. In the Cd0 sample, this manifests as a reduction in domain size and increasingly disordered polarization orientations (Fig. 1b), yielding a slanted P–E loop with reduced hysteresis. The culmination of this evolution is seen in the Cd3 ceramic, where highly dynamic heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) emerge. Here, AFE nanodomains exhibit rapid reversion to their initial state upon field removal, substantially suppressing hysteresis loss and producing a nearly linear P–E loop (Fig. 1c). This transformation is corroborated by atomic-scale HAADF imaging (Fig. 3): while PH displays highly regular antiparallel displacement vectors, Cd3 reveals a mixture of N=4 and N=5 modulation periods with highly disordered magnitudes and directions, confirming the nanoscale local heterogeneity that underpins the relaxor AFE behavior. The disordered modulation structures disrupt long-range AFE ordering, generating polarization discontinuities that trigger bound charge redistribution and local electric field rearrangement, which in turn delays polarization saturation. Thus, the HDSs structure not only substantially improves the E_AF/E_FA characteristics but also effectively minimizes hysteresis loss, demonstrating exceptional potential for designing ceramic materials with superior energy storage performance.
